title: A, an, the, or nothing?
lesson: Use 'a' before a consonant sound and 'an' before a vowel sound for one thing we mention for the first time. Use 'the' when it is clear which thing we mean. Use '—' (no article) for plural or uncountable nouns talked about in general.

items.explanation: Use 'a' before a consonant sound: a cat.
Use 'an' before a vowel sound: an apple.

items.text: Look at {1} sun. It is very bright today.
items.explanation: Use 'the' for something unique: there is only one sun.
Use 'an' before a vowel sound: an elephant. It is capitalized at the start of the sentence.
Use no article for plural nouns talked about in general: I like dogs.
Use 'a' before a consonant sound: a book.

items.text: We saw {1} bird and {2} owl in the garden.
items.explanation: Use 'a' before a consonant sound (a bird) and 'an' before a vowel sound (an owl).
Use 'the' when it is clear which thing we mean: the door of this room.
Use no article with uncountable nouns talked about in general: I drink water.

items.text: There is {1} egg on the plate.
items.explanation: Use 'an' before a vowel sound: an egg.
Use 'the' for something unique: there is only one moon. It is capitalized at the start of the sentence.
Use 'an' before a vowel sound (an orange) and 'a' before a consonant sound (a banana).
